title: Der gescheidte Hans
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 32. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-second tale in the Grimms' collection (KHM 32), a comic “numskull” story driven almost entirely by repetition. Clever Hans courts Grethel, and on each visit brings home a gift he ruins by blindly applying the last errand's advice to the new object: he pockets a goat until it smothers, drags bacon on a rope for the dogs, and finally leads his own bride to the manger and throws real eyes in her face. The humor is the escalating rote formula — the same greetings, the same interrogation, the same “Thut nichts, besser machen” — so read the loops rhythmically and watch each fix misfire. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you meet older forms such as Ermel, Thut, mußtest, gehn and lauft, plus Hans's dialectal telegram-speech (Adies, gegeben han).

sentences.n: This whole tale is built on a rote formula that recurs almost word for word each errand: the farewell (“Machs gut” — “Schon gut machen. Adies, Mutter”), the greeting at Grethel's, and the evening interrogation. Watch it repeat and note how Hans always applies the *last* errand's method to the *new* gift. Hans talks in clipped, subjectless telegram-speech (“Bring nichts,” “Nadel gegeben”), part of the comedy. “Adies” is a dialectal/archaic form of adieu (goodbye).
Hans's telegraphic reply drops the subject: “Schon gut machen” = “[I'll] do [it] well already.”

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.i: “I bring nothing — I want to be given something.”
sentences.n: “gegeben han”: “han” is a dialect form of haben — literally ‘to have given [to me],’ i.e. ‘I want to be given something.’

sentences.t: Grethel schenkt dem Hans eine Nadel.
sentences.n: schenken = to give as a present; dem Hans is dative (‘to Hans’).

sentences.t: Hans nimmt die Nadel, steckt sie in einen Heuwagen und geht hinter dem Wagen her nach Haus.
sentences.l: Hans takes the needle, sticks it into a hay-wagon, and walks behind the wagon home.
sentences.i: Hans takes the needle, sticks it into a hay-wagon, and trudges home behind the wagon.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, Hans. Wo bist du gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Good evening, Hans. Where have you been?”

sentences.n: Parallel to “gegeben han” earlier: “hat” is again a dialect form of haben. Hans tells her he brought nothing but was given something.

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest die Nadel an den Ermel stecken.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have stuck the needle on your sleeve.”
sentences.n: “mußtest” here is a past-tense reproach = ‘you ought to have.’ This ‘Das hast du dumm gemacht … mußtest …’ verdict recurs every errand. “Ermel” is the 1857/archaic spelling of Ärmel (sleeve).
“Thut nichts” (archaic spelling of tut) = ‘it does no matter, never mind.’ This closes each episode — and each time Hans ‘does better’ by wrongly applying this fix to the next gift.

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.t: Grethel schenkte dem Hans ein Messer.
sentences.n: Here schenkte (preterite) rather than the present schenkt used elsewhere — the tellers' small inconsistency, preserved from the 1857 text.

sentences.t: Hans nimmt das Messer, steckts an den Ermel und geht nach Haus.
sentences.l: Hans takes the knife, sticks it on the sleeve, and goes home.
sentences.n: “steckts” = steckt es (sticks it); the pronoun es is clipped onto the verb, as in “schleifts” later.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, Hans. Wo bist du gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Good evening, Hans. Where have you been?”

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest das Messer in die Tasche stecken.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have put the knife in your pocket.”

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.t: Grethel schenkt dem Hans eine junge Ziege.
sentences.l: Grethel gives Hans a young goat.

sentences.t: Hans nimmt die Ziege, bindet ihr die Beine und steckt sie in die Tasche.
sentences.l: Hans takes the goat, binds its legs, and sticks it into his pocket.
sentences.n: Hans applies the *previous* fix (‘put it in your pocket’) to the wrong object — the running joke. “bindet ihr die Beine”: the dative ihr marks whose legs (‘binds the legs for/of it’).

sentences.t: Wie er nach Haus kommt, ist sie erstickt.
sentences.l: By the time he comes home, it has suffocated.
sentences.n: “Wie” here means als/‘when, by the time,’ not ‘how.’ ersticken = to suffocate.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, Hans. Wo bist du gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Good evening, Hans. Where have you been?”

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest die Ziege an ein Seil binden.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have bound the goat to a rope.”

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.t: Grethel schenkt dem Hans ein Stück Speck.
sentences.l: Grethel gives Hans a piece of bacon.

sentences.t: Hans nimmt den Speck, bindet ihn an ein Seil und schleifts hinter sich her.
sentences.l: Hans takes the bacon, binds it to a rope, and drags it along behind him.
sentences.n: “schleifts” = schleift es (drags it). “hinter sich her” = along behind himself.

sentences.t: Die Hunde kommen und fressen den Speck ab.
sentences.l: The dogs come and eat the bacon off.
sentences.i: The dogs come and gobble the bacon clean away.
sentences.n: abfressen (separable: fressen … ab) = to eat off/away; fressen is the verb for animals feeding.

sentences.t: Wie er nach Haus kommt, hat er das Seil an der Hand, und ist nichts mehr daran.
sentences.l: By the time he comes home, he has the rope in his hand, and there is nothing more on it.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, Hans. Wo bist du gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Good evening, Hans. Where have you been?”

sentences.t: „Ans Seil gebunden, heim geführt, Hunde weggeholt.“
sentences.l: “Bound to the rope, led home, dogs carried off.”
sentences.i: “Tied it to the rope, led it home, the dogs made off with it.”

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest den Speck auf dem Kopf tragen.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have carried the bacon on your head.”

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.t: Grethel schenkt dem Hans ein Kalb.
Hans nimmt das Kalb, setzt es auf den Kopf, und das Kalb zertritt ihm das Gesicht.
sentences.l: Hans takes the calf, sets it on his head, and the calf tramples his face.
sentences.n: zertreten = to tread to pieces, trample. “zertritt ihm das Gesicht”: the dative ihm marks the victim — ‘tramples the face for/on him.’

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest das Kalb leiten, und an die Raufe stellen.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have led the calf and set it at the rack.”
sentences.n: Raufe = the hayrack/manger where livestock is tied to feed. Note the fix here — ‘lead it and tie it at the rack’ — which Hans will apply, disastrously, to Grethel herself.

sentences.t: „Guten Tag, Hans. Was bringst du Gutes?“
sentences.l: “Good day, Hans. What good thing do you bring?”

sentences.t: Grethel sagt zum Hans „ich will mit dir gehn.“
sentences.l: Grethel says to Hans, “I will go with you.”
sentences.n: “gehn” = gehen (contracted). Fatally, Grethel offers herself just as Hans has learned to ‘lead and tie at the rack.’

sentences.t: Hans nimmt die Grethel, bindet sie an ein Seil, leitet sie, führt sie vor die Raufe und knüpft sie fest.
sentences.l: Hans takes Grethel, binds her to a rope, leads her, brings her before the rack, and ties her fast.
sentences.n: festknüpfen (separable: knüpft … fest) = to knot/tie fast. Hans follows the calf-instructions to the letter, on his bride.

sentences.t: Darauf geht Hans zu seiner Mutter.
sentences.l: Thereupon Hans goes to his mother.

sentences.t: „Guten Abend, Hans. Wo bist du gewesen?“
sentences.l: “Good evening, Hans. Where have you been?”

sentences.n: mitgehen (separable) = to go along/with. Hans reports Grethel herself as the ‘gift’ that came with him.

sentences.t: „Am Seil geleitet, vor die Raufe gebunden, Gras vorgeworfen.“
sentences.l: “Led on the rope, bound before the rack, threw grass before her.”

sentences.t: „Das hast du dumm gemacht, Hans, mußtest ihr freundliche Augen zuwerfen.“
sentences.l: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have cast her friendly eyes.”
sentences.i: “That you did stupidly, Hans; you should have given her friendly looks.”
sentences.n: Here the literal-application joke turns lethal. The mother means the idiom ‘jemandem freundliche Augen zuwerfen’ — to give someone loving looks (zuwerfen = to throw/cast toward). Hans will take ‘throw eyes at her’ literally in the last scene.

sentences.t: Hans geht in den Stall, sticht allen Kälbern und Schafen die Augen aus und wirft sie der Grethel ins Gesicht.
sentences.l: Hans goes into the stall, pokes out the eyes of all the calves and sheep, and throws them in Grethel's face.
sentences.n: ausstechen (separable: sticht … aus) = to poke/put out. Hans obeys ‘throw her friendly eyes’ to the letter — with real eyes.

sentences.t: Da wird Grethel böse, reißt sich los und lauft fort, und ist Hansens Braut gewesen.
sentences.l: Then Grethel gets angry, tears herself loose and runs away, and has been Hans's bride.
sentences.i: Then Grethel grows angry, tears herself loose and runs off — and that was the last of her being Hans's bride.
sentences.n: “lauft” is the older, un-umlauted form of läuft (runs). The deadpan close “und ist Hansens Braut gewesen” = ‘and [that] was [the end of her being] Hans's bride.’
